items.q: What happens in the recapitulation of sonata form?
items.correct: Both subjects return, but the second subject is now in the tonic key.
items.why: Students miss the KEY RESOLUTION. The exposition put subject 2 in a contrasting key; the recapitulation brings it back to the tonic, resolving the tonal tension - this is the dramatic point of sonata form.

items.correct: A cadence ending on chord V, creating a sense of incompleteness.
items.misconception: A cadence that sounds wrong or is performed badly.
items.random_wrong: A cadence using only major chords.
items.why: Students think 'imperfect' means flawed. It's a technical term for an unfinished-sounding cadence - any chord moving to V leaves the music suspended, awaiting resolution.

items.q: What three notes form a major triad in root position?
items.why: Students think 'three notes = a triad'. Triads are specific: stacked thirds from the root. C major = C, E, G (skipping D and F). Consecutive scale notes are clusters, not triads.

items.q: Whose 1953 Concentus Musicus Wien revived historically informed Baroque playing?
items.why: It's Nikolaus Harnoncourt. “John Eliot Gardiner” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.
The answer examiners want is: Christopher Small. “Susan McClary” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.

items.q: In what year did Glenn Gould abandon live performance?
items.why: The real figure is 1964 AD. The wrong options cluster deliberately close — anchor the exact number to one concrete fact so it can't drift.
